conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility properly, I'll need to gather some information from you.

First, let me read the official guidance on Child Benefit eligibility to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To get started, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions:

**Do you live with Alex in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. Alex lives at home with me, and we live in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. So Alex lives with you in Manchester.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that.

**Is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or in any residential accommodation?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he has been in care for a couple of months, but it's a temporary situation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. This is important information as it can affect Child Benefit eligibility.

**How many weeks has Alex been in care so far?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex has been in care for exactly 8 weeks, I have a follow-up question:

**Does Alex spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you during this time in care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't come home during the week. He's at the care home full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have one more important question:

**Are you making any financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep while he's in care - such as paying for clothes, presents, food, pocket money, or contributing to his accommodation costs?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to contribute financially while he's been in care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I've assessed your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

**Unfortunately, you would not be eligible to continue receiving Child Benefit for Alex at this time.**

Here's why: According to the Child Benefit rules for children in care, you are usually entitled to Child Benefit for up to 8 weeks after a child goes into care. After 8 weeks, you are no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless certain exceptions apply.

The main exceptions are:
- If the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or
- If you regularly spend money on them while they're in care (for things like clothes, presents, food, pocket money, or accommodation)

Since Alex has been in care for exactly 8 weeks (meaning the 8-week entitlement period has ended), and he doesn't spend at least 24 hours a week at home, and you're not currently making financial contributions towards his upkeep, you would not qualify for Child Benefit beyond this initial 8-week period.

**However**, if your circumstances change - for example, if Alex starts coming home regularly for at least 24 hours a week, or if you start making financial contributions towards his upkeep - you may be able to claim Child Benefit again. If that happens, you should contact HMRC to discuss your situation.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: According to the Child Benefit rules for children in care, you are usually entitled to Child Benefit for up to 8 weeks after a child goes into care. After 8 weeks, you are no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless certain exceptions apply. The main exceptions are: (1) if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or (2) if you regularly spend money on them while they're in care. In Alex's case, he has been in care for exactly 8 weeks (which means the 8-week entitlement period has ended), he does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home, and you are not making financial contributions towards his upkeep. Therefore, you would not be eligible to continue receiving Child Benefit for Alex beyond the initial 8-week period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who has been in care for 8 weeks. The claimant lives in the UK and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, under the rules for children in care, Child Benefit normally stops after 8 weeks unless the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home or the parent makes regular financial contributions. Since neither exception applies in this case, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex after the 8-week period has ended.

After 8 weeks, you are no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless certain exceptions apply. In Alex's case, he has been in care for exactly 8 weeks (which means the 8-week entitlement period has ended), he does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home, and you are not making financial contributions towards his upkeep. Therefore, you would not be eligible to continue receiving Child Benefit for Alex beyond the initial 8-week period.

However, under the rules for children in care, Child Benefit normally stops after 8 weeks unless the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home or the parent makes regular financial contributions. Since neither exception applies in this case, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex after the 8-week period has ended.
